perturbation.original_text: (c) Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorised disclosure of Confidential Informat ion and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies , to such compensation as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages shall be the sole and exclusive remedy for any unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information, thereby precluding any other form of relief, including but not limited to injunctive relief or specific performance. The maximum liability of the Receiving Party for any such unauthorized disclosure shall be limited to £1000, regardless of the extent of the damages suffered by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change in Section 3(c) directly contradicts the original agreement's stance on remedies for unauthorized disclosure. Originally, monetary damages were acknowledged as potentially insufficient, allowing for other remedies. The modified text specifies that monetary damages are the *sole* remedy and limits the liability to £1000. This contradicts the intent that Disclosing party shall be entitled to such compensation as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction. By including a limit on liability, it now contradicts what is a reasonable compensation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorised disclosure of Confidential Informat ion and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies , to such compensation as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 3(c) introduces a direct contradiction to the contradicted text. The original text states that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy, whereas the changed text explicitly states that monetary damages 'shall be the sole and exclusive remedy'. Furthermore, the changed text limits the Receiving Party's liability to £1000, which conflicts with the Disclosing Party's entitlement to 'such compensation as may be deemed proper by a court of competent jurisdiction'. The limitation on liability directly clashes with the original expectation of potentially higher, court-determined compensation.

perturbation.original_text: (a) All Confidential Information and Confidential Materials are and shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. By disclosing informatio n to the Receiving Party, Disclosing Party does not grant any express or implied right to Receiving Party to or under Disclosing Party patents, copyrights, trademarks, or trade secret information.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) All Confidential Information and Confidential Materials are and shall become the shared property of both the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party upon disclosure. By disclosing information to the Receiving Party, Disclosing Party grants an implied, royalty-free license to the Receiving Party to use, modify, and create derivative works from such Confidential Information for internal research purposes only. Upon termination of the contract, the Receiving party must hand over anything in their posession.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation in Section 4(a) directly contradicts the original ownership and usage rights of Confidential Information. The original agreement states that all Confidential Information remains the property of the Disclosing Party and grants no rights to the Receiving Party. The modified version states that it becomes shared property and grants an implied license for internal research purposes. This will create contradiction when the Receiving Party must return all original s, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information and/or Confidential Materials, or at Disclosing Party's option, certify destruction of the same in writing to Disclosing Party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (b) Receiving Party shall return all original s, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confidential Information and/or Confidential Materials, or at Disclosing Party's option, certify destruction of the same in writing to Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 4(a) states that Confidential Information becomes the shared property of both parties and grants an implied license to the Receiving Party for internal research. This directly contradicts the contradicted text in Section (b), which requires the Receiving Party to return all copies of Confidential Information to the Disclosing Party or certify their destruction. If the Receiving Party jointly owns the information and has a license to use it, they cannot logically be required to return or destroy all copies. The change introduces a clear conflict in ownership and usage rights, leading to a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (c) This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof. It shall not be modified except by a written agreement dated subsequent to the date of this Agreement and si gned by both parties. None of the provisions of this Agreement shall be deemed to have been waived by any act or acquiescence on the part of the Disclosing Party, its agents, or em ployees, but only by an instrument in writing signed by an authorised officer of Disclosing Party. No waiver of any provision of this Agreement shall constitute a waiver of any othe r provision(s) or of the same provision on another occasion.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof. This agreement can be modified by an email exchange between any employee of the Disclosing Party and Receiving Party, without the need for a formal written agreement. The informal agreement is binding and enforceable. None of the provisions of this Agreement shall be deemed to have been waived by any act or acquiescence on the part of the Disclosing Party, its agents, or employees, but any indication through email shall be considered a valid waiver.
perturbation.explanation: The modification in Section 4(c) contradicts the original clause regarding amendments to the agreement. The original clause requires a written agreement signed by both parties for any modifications. The modified version allows changes via email, contradicting the need for a formal, signed agreement. This creates uncertainty about how modifications will be managed and what constitutes a valid change to the contract. This also now contradicts the need for Disclosing Party may visit Receiving Party's premises, with reasonable prior notice and during normal business hours, to review Receiving Party's compliance with the Terms of this Agreement. What terms would be the Receiving Party following?
perturbation.contradicted_text: (d) Disclosing Party may visit Receiving Party's premises, with reasonable prior notice and during normal business hours, to review Receiving Party's compliance with the Terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The modified Section 4(c) explicitly allows for contract modifications via email, contradicting the original requirement of a 'written agreement dated subsequent to the date of this Agreement and signed by both parties.' This creates a direct conflict. Furthermore, the contradicted text (d) refers to compliance with the 'Terms of this Agreement.' With the introduction of email modifications, it becomes unclear which 'Terms' are valid, as they may be altered informally, thus there is a direct conflict and impact.
